episodes.workingTitle: Diagnose Resolve Playback Lag Before You Change Settings
episodes.targetViewer: Serious solo video creators editing in DaVinci Resolve
episodes.viewerProblem: Resolve playback lag feels random, so solo creators often change settings, blame hardware, or buy upgrades before proving whether the real bottleneck is media, cache, timeline effects, disk, GPU/VRAM, NAS media, or background system load.
episodes.corePromise: Run a repeatable Resolve playback-lag diagnostic and identify the most likely bottleneck before changing random settings, buying hardware, or blaming Resolve.

episodes.thumbnailConcept: STOP GUESSING - Resolve timeline beside Task Manager/Resource Monitor proof panel
episodes.description: Draft planning entry only. Publish metadata is not approved and should not be used for upload prep.
episodes.hook: Show a Resolve timeline stuttering, then Task Manager or Resource Monitor beside it: This is where most lag tutorials go wrong. They jump to settings before proving what is actually slow.
episodes.scriptOutline:
1. The Real Problem: Lag Is a Symptom
Viewer purpose: reframe playback lag as a diagnosis problem, not a settings problem.
Proof/evidence: one stuttering playback example and possible bottleneck classes.
Screen capture requirement: Resolve playback stutter plus visible timeline/playback context.

2. Set Up a Repeatable Lag Test
Viewer purpose: prevent false conclusions from inconsistent testing.
Proof/evidence: one timeline section, one clip, one playback mode, and one timestamped test.
Screen capture requirement: timeline section, playback resolution setting, clip/media details.

3. Check Media and Codec First
Viewer purpose: show why source media can be harder to play than the edit looks.
Proof/evidence: codec/container/media location comparison.
Screen capture requirement: media file properties, Resolve Media Pool, storage location.

4. Watch the System During the Stutter
Viewer purpose: teach signal capture while the problem happens.
Proof/evidence: disk, CPU, GPU/VRAM, and memory observations during playback.
Screen capture requirement: Resolve visible with Task Manager/Resource Monitor beside it.

5. Test Cache, Proxies, or Optimized Media
Viewer purpose: show one targeted workflow fix after evidence points toward decoding/timeline load.
Proof/evidence: before/after playback on the same timeline section.
Screen capture requirement: Resolve cache/proxy/optimized media settings and playback result.

6. Separate Timeline Effects From Media Problems
Viewer purpose: avoid blaming media when Fusion, noise reduction, color nodes, or effects are the load.
Proof/evidence: toggle heavy effects or test a clean timeline segment.
Screen capture requirement: effects/color/Fusion state and comparison playback test.

7. Build the VIDTOOLZ Playback-Lag Checklist
Viewer purpose: give a reusable decision path.
Proof/evidence: checklist for media, location, cache, timeline effects, disk, GPU, background I/O.
Screen capture requirement: checklist overlay or markdown note plus Resolve context.

8. When to Stop and Escalate
Viewer purpose: define when evidence justifies hardware, driver, or project strategy changes.
Proof/evidence: examples of signals that justify next steps and examples that do not.
Screen capture requirement: summary screen with diagnostic outcomes and next actions.
episodes.nextAction: Capture or design the controlled Resolve playback-lag proof test before any script or production prep.
episodes.notes:
Status: idea/draft, not ready to shoot.
Production approval: no.
Package run approved: no.
Shooting approved: no.
Proof required before production: yes.

Proof plan summary:
- Capture Resolve timeline playback stutter with visible timeline context.
- Capture the same timeline after one targeted improvement.
- Capture Project Settings and Timeline Settings relevant to playback resolution, optimized media/cache, and timeline resolution.
- Capture media location and file properties.
- Capture Task Manager Performance and Resource Monitor Disk during playback.
- Capture GPU/VRAM usage during playback if available.
- Capture Resolve cache/proxy/optimized media state before and after the test.

Must not claim without proof:
- One setting fixes all Resolve lag.
- PRESTO hardware is the cause.
- GPU, disk, codec, NAS, or cache is the bottleneck without matching evidence.
- Buying hardware is the next step before workflow/media/cache tests.
- Before/after improvement if timeline conditions differ.

Rejection risks:
- No captured Resolve lag case is available.
- The same test cannot be reproduced before and after a targeted change.
- Monitoring data is too ambiguous to support a clear diagnosis.
- The episode becomes a generic settings roundup instead of an evidence-led workflow.
- The topic overlaps existing tutorials unless the VIDTOOLZ diagnostic framing is clear.

Suggested next action: Capture or design the controlled Resolve playback-lag proof test before any script or production prep.
